data.title: Edgewise Therapeutics downgraded at Scotiabank on heart disease trial results
data.description:
Edgewise Therapeutics (NASDAQ:EWTX) was downgraded to Sector Perform from prior Sector Outperform at Scotiabank after results from the company's mid-stage trial for its heart disease, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

Stock down about 17% in morning trade.

Although a lot of the data was positive, two patients developed two serious adverse events of atrial fibrillation and one patient exited the study after a moderate case of dizziness, as reported by the company.

"Even if we have seen AF in other cardiac myosin inhibitors (CMIs), this still diminishes the main competitive advantage of EDG-7500 (its safety versus CMIs), and these AEs were seen in a small patient population,"  Louise Chen, analyst at Scotiabank said.

EDG-7500 can decrease cardiac outflow gradients and improve diastolic relaxation, without affecting ejection fraction. Although this still appears to be true, the AF side effect has now come up as a potential downside - analyst added.

Scotiabank also cut PT for EWTX to $14 from $50.

data.title: Big Pharma higher after tariff exemption despite ongoing uncertainties
data.description:
Big Pharma was trading higher on Thursday as pharmaceuticals, under pressure from a raft of ongoing industry-specific headwinds, managed to avoid the sweeping new tariff measures the Trump administration has just announced.

VanEck Vectors Pharmaceutical ETF (NASDAQ:PPH), which represents 25 of the leading global drugmakers, gained following a sharp selloff ahead of the tariff announcement, which indicated sharply higher duties against some of the leading trading partners of the U.S.

In reaction, the industry lobby group Pharmaceutical Research and Manufacturers of America (PhRMA) welcomed Trump’s resolve to encourage domestic manufacturing.

“We look forward to working with the administration on ways to ensure America remains the most attractive place in the world to discover and manufacture new treatments and cures,” noted Alex Schriver, PhRMA Senior Vice President of Public Affairs.

However, Big Pharma is not out of the woods yet as the sector continues to navigate the fallout from the abrupt departure of Dr. Peter Marks as the director of the U.S. FDA's Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research (CBER).

A vaccine proponent under whose watch the agency cleared COVID-19 vaccines during the pandemic Marks was forced to resign last week, marking the first high-profile departure under the new HHS secretary, Robert F. Kennedy, Jr., a well-known vaccine skeptic.

During Trump’s second term, the Health and Human Services Department has lost nearly 20,000 employees, including thousands at the FDA, which, according to senior agency leaders, could affect the country’s food and drug inspections.

Meanwhile, despite an exemption from country-specific tariffs on Wednesday, potential pharma-specific tariffs based on the so-called Section 232 national security investigations will continue to weigh on the sector, J.P. Morgan analyst Chris Schott argued in a research note.  

“We expect some level of overhang on the biopharma sector until the Section 232 pharmaceuticals-specific tariffs are announced,” Schott wrote.

Despite the broader tariffs” that Trump has just imposed, pharmaceuticals will continue to be “a relative “safe haven.” Schott argued, noting that biopharma “should be able to manage through tariffs over time given the low capex intensity & high gross margin profile of the sector.

data.title: Stellantis moves to contain tariff fallout, halts production in Mexico and Canada
data.description:
In the wake of President Trump’s Executive Order, Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) quickly went into damage-control mode by announcing it will temporarily lay off 900 U.S. workers and pause production at two assembly plants in Canada and Mexico.

The closures will impact production of the Chrysler Pacifica minivan, Dodge Charger Daytona EV, and Jeep models Compass SUV and Wagoneer EV.

In the U.S., the automaker will pause production at five U.S. plants that provide parts to the shuttered facilities in Canada and Mexico.

In an email to employees, Stellantis’ (NYSE:STLA) head of North American operations attributed the decision to U.S. tariffs on imported vehicles and vehicle components.

“We are continuing to assess the medium- and long-term effects of these tariffs on our operations, but also have decided to take some immediate actions, including temporarily pausing production at some of our Canadian and Mexican assembly plants,” said Stellantis North American COO Antonio Filosa.

Although tariffs will not immediately impact imports from Canada and Mexico, Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) is the most exposed to U.S. trade policy among Detroit’s Big 3. At least 45% of Stellantis’ (NYSE:STLA) U.S. sales come from outside the U.S. And to fund the company’s EV ambitions, former CEO Carlos Tavares shifted much of its production to countries with cheaper labor. This exposure is estimated to impact the company’s bottom line by almost $4B.

Accordingly, Stellantis (STLA) is underperforming peers with a loss of nearly 7%, versus -4% for Ford (F) and -3% for General Motors (GM).

data.title: Dow tariff Teflon stocks: stocks holding up as the Dow plunges 1,500 points
data.description:
As the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) plunges 1,500 points, and is currently about -4.4% year-to-date, we look at the stocks holding up in the index.

CNBC first used the term “Teflon market” to refer to a market that appears to be unaffected by negative news or events, as if not sticking to a Teflon pan.

Coca-Cola (KO) – Up 3.42%

Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) – Up 2.94%

Verizon Communications (VZ) – Up 2.67%

UnitedHealth Group (UNH) – Up 2.43%

Procter & Gamble (PG) – Up 1.85%

McDonald’s (MCD) – Up 1.65%

Merck & Co. (MRK) – Up 1.51%

Amgen (AMGN) – Up 1.01%

data.title: Tariff turmoil: These retail stocks are down more than 15% on recession fears
data.description:
The retail sector is taking some lumps after the Trump Administration announced details on the tariffs that will go into effect this week. The carnage was especially painful with mid-cap and small-cap retail names.

Wayfair (W) -28.5%, Five Below (FIVE) -28.2%, Kohl's (KSS) -23.3%, Gap (GAP) -21.7%, VF Corp (VFC) -21.4%, Victoria's Secret (VSCO) -21.0%, Designer Brands (DBI) -20.0%, American Eagle Outfitters (AEO) -18.5%, Boot Barn (BOOT) -18.2%, Under Armour (UAA) -18.0%, Wolverine World Wide (WWW) -16.9%, Williams-Sonoma (WSM) -16.8%, e.l.f. Beauty (ELF) -15.9%, and Abercrombie & Fitch (ANF) -15.8% were some of the most notable decliners amid growing concerns the U.S. economy is heading toward a recession.

Investors are also starting to dig into which retail companies will be impacted the most based on their current sourcing and manufacturing setups.

The anxiety over a U.S. recession hit hyperdrive after President Trump said the U.S. will levy tariffs at "approximately half" of what the White House has calculated those countries charge for American goods. The U.S. will place a 34% tariff rate on goods from China, 46% rate on Vietnam, 49% on Cambodia, 25% on South Korea, 31% on Switzerland, 32% on Taiwan, 36% on Thailand, 20% on the European Union, 24% on Malaysia, 10% on the U.K. and Brazil, and 24% for Japan. Trump contended that the tariffs will increase competition and bring down prices, although many economists have warned that inflation and recession risks are now increased.

data.title: Nasdaq tumbles 5%, Dow loses 1,500 points as tariffs stoke trade-war fears
data.description:
U.S. stocks suffered sharp losses Thursday, sliding down into correction territory as President Donald Trump's reciprocal tariffs stoked fears of a global trade war. 

The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) sank -5.5%,and the Dow (DJI) fell more than 1,500 points, or down -3.6%. The S&P 500 (SP500) was -4.3%, to 5,429.55.

Trump late Wednesday detailed new tariffs that the U.S. is enacting on imports. The minimum baseline tariff will be 10%, and the highest rate for the latest round of tariffs is 49%.

"In short, the tariffs put in place last night were extraordinary both in terms of scale and in how they were calculated, with President Trump announcing reciprocal tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) as he declared a national emergency over the trade deficit," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said. 

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 15 basis points to 4.05%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 12 basis points to 3.73%.

data.title: Mall, hotel, and office REITs slump after tariff announcement; health REITs gain
data.description:
Hotel, mall and shopping center REITs were among real estate stocks that sank on Thursday after President Donald Trump announced his latest round  of tariffs on Wednesday. With the prospect of higher prices and increased risk of a recession, investors soured on the prospects of travel and consumer spending. Office REITs also wilted on the news.

The sweeping new tariff measures announced by the Trump administration could significantly dampen consumer demand and trigger a sharp slowdown in U.S. retail activity, according to UBS research analyst Amit Mehrotra.

"The slowdown already evident in US economic data (Q1 is tracking slightly negative growth) is more likely to continue after [Wednesday's] tariff announcement," wrote Citi's Andrew Hollenhorst in a note.

Mall REITs Tanger (NYSE:SKT) slumped 9.4%, Simon Property Group (SPG) slid 9.3%, and Macerich (MAC) dipped 9.4% in early Thursday trading. Other shopping Center REITs: CBL & Associates (CBL) -6.4%, Kimco Realty (KIM) -6.0%, EPR Properties (EPR) -4.0%, and Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT) -4.9%.

With continued economic uncertainty, consumers and businesses may be less willing to spend on travel, hurting lodging REITs. Service Properties Trust (NASDAQ:SVC) sank 14%, Apple Hospitality (APLE) -7.7%, Chatham Lodging Trust (CLDT) -8.0%, Pebblebrook Hotel Trust (PEB) -11%.

And just when offices were recovering from the pandemic, the prospect of dimmer economic growth hit. SL Green (SLG) stock tumbled 8.8%, Vornado Realty Trust (VNO) -8.9%, Office Properties income Trust (NASDAQ:OPI) -6.6%, and BXP  (NYSE:BXP) -5.8%.

Another spot to watch for weakness in international trade is industrial REITs. Prologis (NYSE:PLD) stock sank 7.2%, First Industrial Realty Trust (FR) fell 5.3%, and  LXP Industrial Trust (LXP) is off 3.8%.

A recession-resistant sector, health care, offers some green among the sea of red. Omega Healthcare Investors (OHI) +4.2%, Ventas (VTR), +2.0%, Sabra Healthcare (SBRA), +3.0%, and Welltower (WELL), +2.4% traded in positive territory, as did senior housing REIT National Health Investors (NHI), +2.1%.

With the assistance of Editor Mary Christine Joy

data.title: Bassett Furniture outlines 2025 omnichannel growth and cost efficiency strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bassett Furniture Industries (BSET) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Rob Spilman emphasized improved Q1 operating results with a $4.8 million improvement in operating profit due to a streamlined cost structure and increased efficiency in wholesale and retail segments. He highlighted that the company’s strategy for 2025 is focused on managing tepid demand while continuing disciplined growth initiatives.
  • Consolidated sales decreased by 5.1% due to a calendar shift, but normalized sales showed a 2.2% increase. December and January were strong months, partially offset by weaker February sales due to aggressive industry discounting.
  • E-commerce sales grew by 36%, driven by higher conversion rates despite flat web traffic. Investments in omnichannel capabilities, including bassettfurniture.com, are expected to further improve performance.
  • New product launches, including the Andorra and Newberry collections, along with the hideaway BenchMade dining program, are aimed at driving sales. Expansion of the Bassett Custom Studio program, which reached 50 dealers, is another focus.
  • Tariffs have created uncertainty, and management is assessing their potential impacts on pricing and production strategies. 79% of wholesale shipments in Q1 were manufactured or assembled in the US.
  • CFO Dru Ingram reported diluted earnings per share of $0.21 compared to a loss of $0.14 in the prior year, with consolidated operating income of $2.5 million. Gross margins improved by 170 basis points due to better wholesale margins.

Outlook

  • CEO Spilman noted the company’s cautious outlook, with plans to focus on efficient operations and continued investments in omnichannel and marketing to sustain growth.
  • The company plans to incorporate direct mail more frequently into its promotional mix and digital outreach for new product launches and events.
  • Management indicated plans to open new stores in late 2025 or early 2026, in addition to expanding the Bassett Custom Studio program.
  • Tariff impacts are being evaluated, with management engaging with customers and competitors to determine pricing strategies.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated revenue for Q1 declined by $4.4 million due to the additional week in the prior-year quarter. Normalized sales increased by $1.8 million.
  • Wholesale shipments increased by 4.2% on a comparable basis, with notable growth in Lane Venture shipments and the retail store network. Retail delivered sales rose 6.8% on a normalized basis.
  • Gross margins improved by 250 basis points in the wholesale segment, supported by manufacturing efficiency gains and favorable customer mix.
  • SG&A expenses as a percentage of sales decreased by 400 basis points, reflecting the benefits of restructuring and cost reductions.
  • Retail gross margins declined slightly due to more aggressive clearance efforts, but cost savings in marketing and warehouse operations contributed to improved results overall.
  • The company ended Q1 with $56.4 million in cash and no outstanding debt.

Q&A

  • Anthony Lebiedzinski, Sidoti & Company, asked about post-quarter trends and consumer confidence. CEO Spilman noted that while business improved in December and January, it has since returned to prior-year levels.
  • Lebiedzinski inquired about inventory levels and tariffs. Spilman stated that inventory increases were tied to preparations for the spring season and were not directly related to tariffs, though the recent tariff announcement may influence future decisions.
  • Brian Gordon, Water Tower Research, asked about gross margins and their sustainability. Spilman explained that while current margins are among the highest achieved, they are unlikely to increase further, with moderate adjustments expected to pricing strategies and inventory management.
  • Gordon also inquired about new store openings and the Bassett Custom Studio program. Spilman confirmed plans for two new leases and significant potential for expanding the studio program.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns about tariffs and their impact on pricing strategies, as well as the sustainability of gross margins. The tone was neutral to slightly pressing, reflecting uncertainty in the external environment.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in addressing operational improvements and growth plans but acknowledged challenges from tariffs and consumer confidence. Spilman emphasized the company’s flexibility and strategic focus.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained consistent, reflecting confidence in cost controls and strategic initiatives despite external headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained cautious, with a continued focus on efficiency and growth through omnichannel and new product launches. The addition of new whole-home collections and a push for e-commerce growth marked a strategic shift.
  • Analysts maintained focus on gross margins and tariff impacts, reflecting ongoing industry challenges.
  • Key financial metrics improved, including normalized sales growth and gross margin expansion. The tone and confidence levels of management were consistent with the prior quarter, demonstrating stability in navigating market conditions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariffs were highlighted as a significant challenge, with potential impacts on pricing and production strategies. Management is actively engaging with stakeholders to mitigate risks.
  • Consumer confidence and economic data pose risks to demand, as noted by Spilman in his remarks about the broader furniture market.
  • The competitive promotional environment in the industry adds pressure on margins.

Final Takeaway

Bassett Furniture Industries reported improved operating results in Q1 2025, driven by cost efficiency and growth in e-commerce. The company is focused on navigating market challenges, including tariffs and consumer confidence, while investing in omnichannel capabilities and new product lines to sustain growth. Strategic initiatives such as the Bassett Custom Studio program and direct mail marketing indicate a proactive approach to enhancing customer engagement and expanding market presence.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Deutsche Bank spotlights recession talks and rate cuts as Trump’s tariffs hit markets
data.description:
Deutsche Bank has raised concerns that President Donald Trump’s recent tariff measures, dubbed 'Liberation Day,' could escalate recession risks and potentially prompt rate cuts sooner rather than later, should these tariffs remain in place for an extended period.

Regarding the idea of rate cuts, the investment institution stated: “For the Fed this makes them more likely to cut even if the direction of travel is highly stagflationary. The bias will now be towards up to four cuts this year if this tariff policy holds.” 

Moreover, from the idea of a recession, Deutsche Bank added: “Recession risks will likely rise materially if these tariffs are sustained.” 

The investment bank highlighted that President Trump’s latest tariff measures, combined with mounting concerns about consumer confidence and the outlook from corporate executives, create a significantly more difficult environment for forecasting on Wall Street. As uncertainty continues to cloud the markets, navigating this landscape has become increasingly complex, further compounding the challenges faced by analysts and investors alike. 

Market tracking ETFs: (NYSEARCA:DIA), (NYSEARCA:DDM), (NYSEARCA:DOG), (NYSEARCA:DXD), (NYSEARCA:SDOW), (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), (NYSEARCA:SPXU), (NASDAQ:QQQ), (NASDAQ:QQQM), (NYSEARCA:QLD), (NASDAQ:TQQQ), (NYSEARCA:QID), and (NASDAQ:SQQQ).

data.title: US Treasuries will continue to fall amid tariff announcements – MS
data.description:
Investors sold U.S. Treasuries shortly after 4 p.m. ET on Wednesday as a reaction to President Donald Trump’s across-the-board 10% tariffs on all imports.

A little before 4:30 p.m. ET, U.S. Treasuries reversed their prior weakness, with 7-year (US7Y) and 10-year (US10Y) yields falling about 10 basis points.

“We think this initial reaction was because the size of the tariffs was smaller than investors had expected, providing a respite from a further deterioration in growth expectations,” said Michael Zezas, Morgan Stanley head of U.S. public policy research.

However, “we think larger – and indications of a more lasting – tariffs, relative to the prior status quo, will now weigh further on investor growth expectations,” he added.

Also, Morgan Stanley economists do not expect the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates this year at all, and given this environment, Zezas said he sees “further scope for yields to fall as the U.S. Treasury market continues to trade with a ‘fewer cuts now, more later’ mantra.”

The 30-year Treasury (US30Y) is currently 4.47%

The 10-year Treasury (US10Y) is currently 4.03%

The 2-year Treasury (US2Y) is currently 3.71%

The 3-month Treasury (US3M) is currently 4.29%

Here is the rest of the bonds and their performances.
